summary: Drew DeVault announces Wio, a Wayland compositor inspired by Plan 9's Rio desktop environment, built on wlroots. Each application runs in its own nested Wayland compositor using Cage, enabling Rio's distinctive window-within-window behavior. He argues this demonstrates how Wayland's conservative design enables use-cases impossible on X11. The project adds modern features like multihead and HiDPI support while remaining incomplete on Rio compatibility, and he invites contributors to help.
key_points: Wio recreates Plan 9's Rio desktop paradigm on Wayland by nesting each application in its own Wayland compositor via Cage
Wayland's fundamentally different architecture enables window composition patterns that are impossible on X11
wlroots makes features like multihead, HiDPI, and layer shell support easy — things that would require kernel hacking on Plan 9
Key missing features include a Rio-like window management filesystem (potentially via FUSE) and per-application namespaces
money_quotes: Wio is a Wayland compositor based on wlroots which has a similar look and feel to Plan 9's Rio desktop.
It works by running each application in its own nested Wayland compositor, based on Cage - yet another wlroots-based Wayland compositor.
This is a good demonstration of how Wayland's fundamentally different and conservative design allows for some interesting use-cases which aren't possible at all on X11.
I've also given Wio some nice features which are easy thanks to wlroots, but difficult on Plan 9 without kernel hacking.
Layer shell support is particularly cool, since programs like swaybg and waybar work on Wio.
key_insight: Wayland's compositional architecture enables Plan 9-style nested window management that was fundamentally impossible under X11, vindicating its conservative redesign.
